summary: A collection of letters from Elizabeth Anscombe to G.H. von Wright in 1951, detailing their early efforts as literary executors of Ludwig Wittgenstein's estate. The letters reveal Anscombe's pragmatic approach to publishing the Philosophical Investigations, her protective stance against unauthorized publications, and her intellectual humility regarding editorial decisions outside her expertise.

principles.statement: Fulfill an author's wishes pragmatically, not rigidly.
principles.rationale: While executors should make every effort to carry out an author's publication wishes, practical constraints like book size, cost, and aesthetic readability can justify deviating from the exact original plan.
principles.source_quote: Though I think it right to make every effort to carry out Wittgenstein’s wishes, I shall not actually be upset if the Tractatus can not be printed with the Untersuchungen. Largely because the book will look a bit odd, starting with a solid German text and then going into German-cum-English; and the Tractatus will certainly be available to anyone wanting to read the Untersuchungen.

principles.statement: Publicly assert authority to deter unauthorized publications.
principles.rationale: When unauthorized individuals attempt to publish an author's private notes, executors must issue public warnings in major journals to clarify the author's actual instructions and assert copyright.
principles.source_quote: I think we may have to make some sort of announcement via a letter to the Times or Mind, both saying that Wittgenstein left instructions to publish (for the excuse of these people is that he wished to withhold his work from the world), and giving a warning about copyright.

principles.rationale: When dealing with complex material outside one's domain, it is better to admit a lack of qualification and defer to others than to make poor editorial decisions.
principles.source_quote: I am so utterly unqualified to help in coming to any decision about the mathematical work.

signature_quotes.text: Though I think it right to make every effort to carry out Wittgenstein’s wishes, I shall not actually be upset if the Tractatus can not be printed with the Untersuchungen.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the failure to secure rights to reprint the Tractatus alongside the Philosophical Investigations.

signature_quotes.text: Flew is too young to make a shady proposal sound anything but shady, but some more cunning person may try it on with some other publisher.
signature_quotes.context: Reacting to Anthony Flew's unauthorized attempt to publish Wittgenstein's Blue and Brown Books.

signature_quotes.text: I am so utterly unqualified to help in coming to any decision about the mathematical work.
signature_quotes.context: Deferring to others regarding the publication of Wittgenstein's mathematical manuscripts.

anti_patterns.description: Making delicate threats to uncooperative publishers.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Legal counsel advised that intimating a threat, however delicately, would only 'get their hackles up' rather than force them to concede copyright permissions.
